preamble: L-vocalization is the process by which an alveolar lateral approximant [l] is replaced by a labio-velar approximant [w] or a similar vowel sound. It typically occurs in coda positions where the lateral closure is lost, leaving only the velarization.

examples.note: Historical 'dark l' (ł) became [w] in modern standard Polish (e.g., 'mały' [mawɨ]).
Common in many modern British dialects in syllable-final position.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
